subject: Robes, clothes, and cloth armor
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 036 (not the heuristic)
note: All 28 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content extracted from the transcript. Cloth/robes need no proficiency (so any class, esp. casters/monks, competes for them) and are rated by power when acquired.

Graceful Cloth.why: +2 Dexterity (the best stat: AC, initiative, attack/damage) plus advantage on Dex saves and +5ft jump — the author calls it the single strongest item on the whole list, best-in-slot for many party members all game.

Protecty Sparkswall.why: +1 spell save DC (the number-one caster stat) on the earliest robe that grants it, plus a minor lightning-charge AC/save bonus; an unassuming Act 1 item that stays best-in-slot for robe-wearing casters through Acts 1-2.

Potent Robe.why: +1 AC, adds your Charisma modifier to cantrip damage (multiplied across Eldritch Blast beams), and grants Charisma-modifier temp HP each turn — best-in-slot for warlocks and great on any caster; every ability is excellent.

Bonespike Garb.why: 15 temp HP on entering rage, -2 incoming damage, and Con-modifier piercing retaliation; superb on Barbarians and even better on an abjuration wizard (the damage reduction stacks nonlinearly with Arcane Ward) — the best default cloth pick.

Robe of the Weave.why: +1 spell save DC and spell attack rolls plus +2 AC (the highest from a cloth set) — a strict upgrade over the Protecty Sparkswall and the best-in-slot Act 3 robe for wizards and sorcerers.

Vest of Soul Rejuvenation.why: +2 AC (one of only two cloth sets that grant it), 1d4 heal on a spell save, and a reaction unarmed strike when missed — best-in-slot for monks, though somewhat S-by-default since late-game monk clothing is thin.

Infernal Robe.why: +1 AC, fire resistance, and Fire Shield (warm, also granting cold resistance) once/rest — one of the earliest robes that raises AC with two relevant defensive abilities, but story-locked and defense-focused when casters usually want offense.

Enraging Heart Garb.why: +2 Constitution (more HP and better concentration saves, especially valuable for casters); its Wrath-on-rage ability is bugged and does nothing, but the passive +2 Con alone makes it a solid defensive pick.

Icebite Robe.why: Cold resistance plus Armour of Agathys at level 3 once/rest (15 temp HP + 15 cold retaliation) that you can cast then unequip — effectively a free 3rd-level Agathys scroll daily, great for abjuration wizards or anyone wanting temp HP.

Moon Devotion Robe.why: +1 to all saves, advantage on Con saves, free Mage Armor, and at-will Produce Flame with radiant retaliation on saves — probably the best purely-defensive robe in Act 2 for casters maintaining concentration.

Garb of the Land and Sky.why: +1 AC, Blade Ward while using Patient Defense, and (via a bug) advantage + 1d8 force on all attacks the turn you use Step of the Wind — a nice monk bonus on a move you'd make anyway, though it costs a Flurry of Blows.

Bloodguzzler Garb.why: Two stacks of Wrath (+1 melee damage/turn, up to +7) whenever an enemy damages you — powerful for multi-attack melee, but you must get hit, it doesn't work on unarmed strikes, and better Wrath sources exist; a build-around item.

Bided Time.why: Arcane Charge (+2 spell damage on threatened enemies) when struck by a melee attack; great for an abjuration wizard's Magic Missile/Scorching Ray, but unreliable to trigger for anyone who isn't deliberately eating hits.

Mighty Cloth.why: +2 Strength (the Graceful Cloth's strength counterpart) plus advantage on Str checks and a push ability; a top item if not for the Giant Strength elixirs, which set strength higher and free the slot — so only for non-elixir strength builds.

Robe of Exquisite Focus.why: +1 spell save DC, but it arrives much later than the strictly-better Protecty Sparkswall and you'll have other save-DC items by then — only used if you run two casters who both want save DC in the chest slot.

Mutilated Carapace.why: +1 attack/damage while shapeshifted or disguised, +1 AC, and advantage on Deception/Persuasion; a fine multi-attack bonus (esp. Eldritch Blast) but you must stay disguised, and the abilities don't synergize into anything exciting.

Robe of Supreme Defences.why: Adds your spellcasting modifier to ALL saves while concentrating (so you basically never fail) plus +1 AC — the best defensive robe in Act 3, but defense is the wrong direction by then, so it's rarely needed.

Wavemother's Robe.why: Fire AND cold resistance, 1d4 heal/turn while standing in water, a 4th-level Create/Destroy Water once/rest, and +1 AC — a cohesive bundle of defensive options to swap in for elemental fights or to set up water synergies.

Armour of Uninhibited Kushigo.why: Monk-only: an unarmed strike against attackers who miss you while in Patient Defense — but monks have low AC (few misses), rarely want to use Patient Defense, and it does nothing for other classes; a weak build-around at best.

Obsidian Laced Robe.why: Fire resistance (the most common damage type, with many single-type fire encounters) plus minor fire retaliation on spell saves — a reasonable swap-in for a known fire fight, but it costs your whole chest slot.

Cloth of Authority.why: +1 AC, advantage on Intimidation and the otherwise-unboostable Insight checks, and fear immunity; each ability is just okay and easily duplicated, so the bundle never makes a powerful item — mainly for the Insight advantage if you lack fear immunity.

Drunken Cloth.why: While drunk: advantage on Con saves and Con-modifier temp HP each turn — useful for a wall-of-fire evocation wizard taking constant chip damage, but situational, defense-only, and it costs a bonus action/pre-combat drink.

Shelter of Athkatla.why: Advantage on saves vs spells plus Mirror Image once/rest; the Robe of Supreme Defences far outclasses the save bonus, and Mirror Image is easy to get from scrolls, so the item-swapping hassle isn't worth it.

Poisoner's Robe.why: +1d4 on poison-damage spells, but almost no spells deal poison damage (mostly just Ray of Sickness and Cloud Kill), so even a Cloud Kill build would rather have save DC or initiative — no scenario where it beats an alternative.

Robe of Summer.why: Just cold resistance, an effect that's narrow (mostly enemy-caster damage), better gained from elixirs/spells, and costs a whole gear slot to swap in — it looks great, which is the only reason to wear it.

Cloth Armour.why: An Easter-egg/cut-content broken Bloodguzzler Garb (same Wrath-on-damage effect) found in Act 3; that effect was mediocre in Act 1 and is far worse this late.

Veil of the Morning.why: Turn Undead once/rest (always uses Wisdom, so weak for non-Wis casters) plus undead disadvantage on attacks against you and advantage on saves vs them; only ~1.5 better than +2 AC even in undead fights, and purely defensive/situational.

Viconia's Priestess Robe.why: +1 AC, advantage on stealth while obscured, and a beefed-up Shield of Faith — but Shield of Faith is a garbage concentration pick this late, and the Graceful Cloth does everything here far better; weak and uncohesive.
